text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- URGENT FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Progressive Disease on Second-Line Therapy / Goals of Care Discussion / Third-Line Planning

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Gerald T. Holloway is a 59-year-old male with known stage IVA (pT3 pN2a M1a) KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with hepatic and pulmonary metastases, presenting today for an urgent office visit following review of his most recent restaging CT scan, which demonstrates unambiguous disease progression on FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (second-line therapy). He was contacted by the clinic after radiology review and asked to come in for urgent discussion.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Holloway was initially diagnosed approximately 19 months ago with synchronous metastatic sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, presenting with a 4.8 cm sigmoid primary and four hepatic metastases (largest 3.1 cm in segment VI). He underwent laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with R0 resection (pT3 pN2a, 3/18 positive nodes, LVI and PNI present), followed by first-line systemic therapy with mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab. His disease achieved a meaningful partial response on FOLFOX/bevacizumab -- hepatic lesions decreased by roughly 50-55% in aggregate -- but oxaliplatin was ultimately discontinued after cycle 10 due to progressive grade 2-3 peripheral sensory neuropathy (persistent bilateral hand and foot paresthesias with functional impairment). He transitioned to maintenance 5-fluorouracil plus bevacizumab.

While on maintenance, disease remained relatively stable through approximately 7 months but then progressed radiographically at approximately the 10-month mark, with modest hepatic lesion growth and the appearance of two new right-sided pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 0.8 cm, right upper lobe 0.5 cm). CEA rose concurrently from 22 to 41 ng/mL. At that point, the molecular profile was reviewed: KRAS G12D mutation confirmed by prior NGS, MSS status, BRAF V600E wild-type, NRAS wild-type -- making him ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy, BRAF-targeted combinations, or immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy. He was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab.

He initially responded well to FOLFIRI/ramucirumab, with partial response on cycle 6 restaging (hepatic and pulmonary lesions decreased, CEA dropped from 41 to 26 ng/mL). Disease remained in a stable/partial response state through the cycle 12 restaging approximately 6 months into second-line therapy (CEA 22 ng/mL, CA 19-9 19 U/mL). However, his course was complicated by grade 3 hypertension attributed primarily to ramucirumab at approximately cycle 8-9, requiring ramucirumab to be held for one cycle and the addition of a third antihypertensive agent (hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg); blood pressure subsequently improved and ramucirumab was resumed at full dose.

At the cycle 16 office visit approximately 3 months ago, his disease remained controlled per CT (stable disease), though he was noted to have mild anemia (hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL), cumulative fatigue, and mildly elevated transaminases (ALT 52, AST 48).

Today's visit is prompted by his cycle 18 restaging CT, completed within the last week. Imaging demonstrates clear progressive disease after approximately 9 months total on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab: hepatic metastases have grown modestly across all four segments, the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule has grown from 0.7 to 0.9 cm, and tumor markers have risen substantially -- CEA now 58 ng/mL (up from 22) and CA 19-9 47 U/mL (up from 19). No new sites of distant metastatic disease were identified (no peritoneal disease, no bone, no brain).

Mr. Holloway came in today with his wife. He reports increased fatigue over the past 4-6 weeks, which he had been attributing to the chemotherapy. He also notes mild increase in abdominal bloating and a 4-pound unintentional weight loss over the past month. He denies new pain, cough, dyspnea, hemoptysis, melena, or hematochezia. Neuropathy from prior oxaliplatin has continued to slowly improve and is now grade 1 (mild, persistent distal foot numbness, not functionally limiting). He remains ambulatory and independent in all activities of daily living but acknowledges he is more tired than he was 6 months ago.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Increased fatigue (significant), 4 lb unintentional weight loss over past month. No fevers, no night sweats, no chills.
Gastrointestinal: Mild abdominal bloating, no pain, no change in bowel habits, no nausea, no vomiting, no melena, no hematochezia. Bowel function has been relatively normal since colectomy (1-3 formed stools per day, mild looser consistency on FOLFIRI days, otherwise stable).
Pulmonary: No cough, no dyspnea at rest, no dyspnea on exertion beyond mild exertional fatigue. No hemoptysis.
Neurological: Grade 1 residual peripheral sensory neuropathy in bilateral feet (improved from peak grade 2-3 on oxaliplatin). No new neurological symptoms.
Musculoskeletal: Mild diffuse muscle aching, likely fatigue-related. No focal bone pain.
Cardiovascular: Blood pressure managed on three antihypertensives. No chest pain, no palpitations, no edema.
Dermatologic: Mild scattered acneiform changes on face and upper chest, present since starting ramucirumab, not bothersome.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, no hematuria.
Endocrine/metabolic: No new symptoms.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Mr. Holloway is a 59-year-old male, appearing mildly fatigued but in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Appears slightly more cachectic than at his last visit 3 months ago.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 144/90 mmHg (right arm, seated, repeated -- 141/88 mmHg), heart rate 76 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 36.7 degrees C, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight 81.2 kg (down from 82.8 kg at last visit; baseline at diagnosis approximately 86 kg -- 5.5 kg total weight loss since diagnosis).
ECOG Performance Status: 1-2 (ambulates and capable of self-care, unable to do any work activities; up and about more than 50% of waking hours).
Head/Neck: No scleral icterus. Mucous membranes moist. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpated. No temporal wasting noted.
Chest/Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, crackles, or decreased breath sounds. No dullness to percussion.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 normal. No S3/S4. No murmurs. No lower extremity edema.
Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended (subjectively more than baseline). Mild tenderness to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant without guarding or rebound. Surgical scar from prior laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy well-healed. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly by percussion (note: hepatic metastases are small and not expected to produce clinically palpable organomegaly). No ascites by shifting dullness or fluid wave.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. Pulses 2+ throughout.
Neurological: Alert and cooperative. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Decreased light touch and proprioception in bilateral feet (toes and distal foot), consistent with residual oxaliplatin-related neuropathy. Gait stable and unassisted.
Skin: Mild scattered papulopustular acneiform eruption on bilateral cheeks and upper chest/shoulders -- grade 1. No significant skin breakdown or superinfection.

---

LABORATORY DATA (from today's draw and most recent values):
- CEA: 58 ng/mL (rising; 22 ng/mL at cycle 12, 24 ng/mL cycle 9, 26 ng/mL at cycle 6)
- CA 19-9: 47 U/mL (rising; 19 U/mL at cycle 12)
- CBC: WBC 4.6 x10^9/L, ANC 2,300/uL, hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL (mild anemia, slightly worsened from 10.8 at last visit), MCV 88 fL, platelets 189 x10^9/L
- BMP: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 3.9 mEq/L, creatinine 0.94 mg/dL (eGFR >60), BUN 16 mg/dL, glucose 102 mg/dL
- LFTs: ALT 61 U/L (mildly elevated, was 52), AST 55 U/L (mildly elevated, was 48), alkaline phosphatase 112 U/L (mildly elevated, was 98), total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL (normal), albumin 3.3 g/dL (mildly low, was 3.6 at diagnosis)
- PT/INR: 1.1 (normal)
- Magnesium: 1.8 mg/dL (low-normal)
- Urinalysis: No significant proteinuria (1+ protein, trace; within acceptable range for patient on anti-angiogenic therapy)

---

IMAGING REVIEW:
Restaging CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV contrast (most recent, reviewed today):

Hepatically, all four previously identified metastatic lesions have grown compared to the prior cycle 12 scan: the segment VI lesion -- which has been the dominant lesion throughout his course -- is now 1.5 cm (up from 1.3 cm on cycle 12 CT, and down from its original 3.1 cm at diagnosis); the segment IVb lesion is 1.2 cm (up from 1.0 cm); the segment II lesion is 0.6 cm (up from 0.5 cm); and the segment VII lesion is 0.4 cm (up from 0.3 cm). The hepatic lesions remain relatively small in absolute size but the pattern of growth across all four is consistent, and when combined with the marker trajectory, clearly represents progressive disease. No new hepatic lesions are identified.

In the lung, the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule has grown from 0.7 to 0.9 cm; the right upper lobe nodule is 0.5 cm (stable). No new pulmonary lesions. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy.

The sigmoid colectomy bed is unremarkable with no evidence of local recurrence. No peritoneal implants. No ascites. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No bone lesions identified. Adrenal glands unremarkable.

Overall pattern: steady growth across existing hepatic and pulmonary sites with rising tumor markers -- consistent with disease that has escaped control on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab.

---

ASSESSMENT:
1. Stage IVA (synchronous hepatic and pulmonary metastatic) sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma -- progressive disease on second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab, after approximately 9 months of second-line therapy (cycles 1-18). Prior therapies: first-line mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab (partial response, limited by grade 2-3 oxaliplatin neuropathy, approximately 10 cycles total oxaliplatin-containing then switched to maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab), maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab (progression at approximately 10 months total from first-line start), second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (partial response then progressive disease at cycle 18).

   Molecular profile: KRAS G12D mutant (confirmed on both primary tumor biopsy/colectomy specimen and prior NGS), MSS, BRAF V600E wild-type, NRAS wild-type, SMAD4 wild-type, PIK3CA wild-type, TMB-low (6 mut/Mb). This profile makes the patient definitively ineligible for: anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab -- KRAS mutant), BRAF-targeted combinations (BRAF wild-type), and standard immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy (MSS). No NTRK fusions, no RET fusions, no HER2 amplification per prior comprehensive NGS.

   ECOG PS 1-2 -- some functional decline from ECOG 1 at prior visit, attributable to disease progression and cumulative therapy-related fatigue.

2. Residual grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy -- slowly improving off oxaliplatin.

3. Hypertension, grade 1-2 -- controlled on triple antihypertensive therapy (lisinopril 20 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg); blood pressure today mildly elevated at 144/90. Continue current regimen. Monitor closely if anti-angiogenic agents continued in next line.

4. Mild anemia (Hb 10.2 g/dL) -- consistent with anemia of chronic disease in the setting of ongoing malignancy. No active bleeding by history. No iron deficiency pattern (MCV 88). No intervention indicated at this time.

5. Mild transaminase elevation (ALT 61, AST 55, ALP 112) with mild hypoalbuminemia (3.3 g/dL) -- likely multifactorial (hepatic metastases, cumulative chemotherapy effect, nutritional decline). Total bilirubin normal. Hepatic synthetic function preserved (INR normal). These values are within acceptable range for further systemic therapy but warrant monitoring.

6. Mild unintentional weight loss (4 lbs over 1 month, approximately 5.5 kg from diagnosis baseline) -- nutritional support referral to be placed. Megace versus mirtazapine for appetite stimulation discussed; deferred for now pending treatment planning.

---

PLAN:

Disease Management -- Third-Line Systemic Therapy:
Mr. Holloway and his wife were both present for this discussion, which was held at length. I explained clearly that the CT results show that the current treatment regimen (FOLFIRI/ramucirumab) is no longer holding the cancer in check -- the liver spots and lung spots have grown and the tumor markers have risen significantly. He understands this means we need to change treatment.

We reviewed the following options in the context of his molecular profile and treatment history:

(a) TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, brand name Lonsurf): An oral chemotherapy approved for refractory metastatic colorectal cancer. Given twice daily on days 1-5 and 8-12 of each 28-day cycle. Main side effects include myelosuppression (particularly neutropenia), fatigue, nausea. The addition of bevacizumab to TAS-102 (as in the SUNLIGHT trial) has shown improved survival outcomes compared to TAS-102 alone in the refractory setting. Given he has previously been exposed to bevacizumab (not to ramucirumab specifically, though both target VEGF pathway), bevacizumab re-challenge in combination with TAS-102 is a reasonable consideration, pending blood pressure stability.

(b) Regorafenib (Stivarga): An oral multikinase inhibitor approved in the refractory colorectal setting. Given daily on days 1-21 of each 28-day cycle, typically with dose escalation per ReDOS strategy starting at 80 mg/day. Side effects include hand-foot skin reaction, fatigue, hypertension, hepatotoxicity, and diarrhea. Given his existing hypertension management needs and mildly elevated transaminases, regorafenib carries somewhat more concern, but remains a legitimate option.

(c) Clinical trial -- KRAS G12D-directed investigational therapy: The patient's KRAS G12D mutation has historically been considered undruggable, but several KRAS G12D-selective small molecule inhibitors are now in early clinical development (Phase I/II). I discussed with Mr. Holloway the possibility of enrolling in a clinical trial targeting KRAS G12D. There is a Phase I/II trial currently open at a regional cancer center approximately 45 minutes from his home, evaluating an investigational oral KRAS G12D inhibitor in patients with KRAS G12D-mutant solid tumors including colorectal cancer. Prior lines of therapy (2 lines completed) would make him potentially eligible, pending full screening. This represents an opportunity to access novel, molecularly targeted therapy tailored to his specific driver mutation, with the potential for meaningful disease control. The experimental nature and uncertainties of Phase I/II trial participation were explained in full.

After thorough discussion, Mr. Holloway expressed strong preference to pursue clinical trial enrollment as his next step -- he is motivated, has a KRAS G12D mutation confirmed on both primary and metastatic tissue, understands the investigational nature of the treatment, and is willing to travel. He would like TAS-102 (with or without bevacizumab) held in reserve as a subsequent option if trial therapy fails or he becomes ineligible.

Plan: Referral to the clinical trial unit at the regional cancer center will be placed today. I will contact the trial coordinator directly. The patient will undergo re-biopsy of an accessible metastatic lesion (right lower lobe pulmonary nodule, amenable to CT-guided biopsy) as required for trial enrollment re-biopsy/correlative studies -- referral to interventional radiology placed for CT-guided lung biopsy, to be scheduled at the trial site or here depending on trial protocol.

Goals of Care:
I had a frank, compassionate discussion with Mr. Holloway and his wife today regarding the overall disease trajectory. He has now progressed on two lines of standard systemic therapy. His disease remains limited (hepatic and pulmonary metastases only, no peritoneal or bone involvement) and his performance status, though slightly declined from peak, remains reasonable (ECOG 1-2). He confirms his primary goal remains disease control with preservation of quality of life. He has thought about the future and acknowledges that at some point, the balance of burden of treatment versus benefit may shift. He is not ready to discuss hospice at this time, and this is entirely appropriate given remaining treatment options. We discussed that if additional therapies are not providing benefit or his performance status declines further, we will revisit goals of care at that time. He was reassured that palliative and supportive care services remain available at any point.

Patient was given printed information on TAS-102/Lonsurf as a backup option and information about clinical trial participation. He and his wife were encouraged to call with any questions. His wife expressed gratitude for the thorough explanation.

Hypertension:
Blood pressure 144/90 today -- slightly above target. Current regimen: lisinopril 20 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily. Will continue current regimen. If anti-angiogenic agent continued in next line, very close BP monitoring warranted. If BP worsens off ramucirumab (which it may improve on cessation), medication adjustments can be made.

Nutrition:
Referral placed to oncology dietitian for nutritional counseling given 4 lb weight loss and mildly low albumin. High-calorie, high-protein diet encouraged. Oral nutritional supplementation (e.g., Ensure Plus or Boost High Protein) recommended in the interim.

Neuropathy:
Grade 1 residual, slowly improving. No new intervention required. Continue monitoring.

Anemia:
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, stable pattern of mild anemia of chronic disease. No transfusion or erythropoiesis-stimulating agent indicated at this time. Recheck with next labs.

Transaminases:
Mild elevation, trending upward slowly. Will recheck at next visit. If further elevation, will assess for hepatic progression versus medication effect versus nutritional depletion.

Follow-up:
Patient will be contacted by the clinical trial coordinator within 2-3 business days. CT-guided lung biopsy to be scheduled within 1-2 weeks. If trial enrollment screening proceeds, next visit will be at the regional cancer center trial site. Routine oncology follow-up here in 4 weeks or sooner as needed.

Time spent with patient and family: 55 minutes, majority in counseling, goals of care discussion, and care coordination.

Electronically signed,
Attending Hematology/Medical Oncology
Hematology/Medical Oncology
